perturbation.original_text: Supplier will confirm or reject Purchase Orders within [***] of receipt of the Purchase Order. Orders not rejected in writing within such time will be deemed confirmed and accepted by Supplier.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will confirm or reject Purchase Orders within 1 hour of receipt of the Purchase Order. Orders not rejected in writing within such time will be deemed confirmed and accepted by Supplier.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confirmation/rejection timeframe to 1 hour is likely insufficient and unreasonable, potentially violating general principles of contract law that require a reasonable opportunity to review and respond to offers (Purchase Orders).
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Time for Acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law dictates that acceptance of an offer must occur within a reasonable time. A one-hour window may not provide sufficient time for the Supplier to adequately assess the Purchase Order, particularly considering factors such as the complexity of the order, internal approval processes, and workload, potentially rendering the acceptance invalid.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text sets a timeframe of 1 hour for the supplier to confirm or reject Purchase Orders, which contradicts general principles of contract law requiring a reasonable opportunity to review and respond to offers. The 'Law Explanation' states a one-hour window may not provide sufficient time for the Supplier to adequately assess the Purchase Order. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation and the law explanation clearly point to the unreasonableness of the timeframe making the contract terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement for cause if the other Party fails to perform any material provision of this Agreement or commits a material breach of this Agreement which is not corrected within [***] after receiving written notice of the failure or breach. except that if the default is by 6 Supplier that creates an immediate public food safety risk, PNC may terminate this Agreement immediately without regard to any period for correction.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement for cause if the other Party fails to perform any material provision of this Agreement or commits a material breach of this Agreement which is not corrected within 1 day after receiving written notice of the failure or breach. except that if the default is by 6 Supplier that creates an immediate public food safety risk, PNC may terminate this Agreement immediately without regard to any period for correction.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 1 day to correct a material breach may be deemed unreasonable and conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC's implied duty of good faith requires that parties act honestly and fairly in their dealings. Providing only one day to cure a material breach may be considered a violation of this duty, especially if the breach requires more time to rectify, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the cure period for a material breach to just one day, which is a significant reduction from the original unspecified timeframe. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be unreasonable and conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation also supports this, stating that providing only one day to cure a material breach may violate the duty of good faith, especially if more time is needed to rectify the breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing UCC Â§ 1-304, confirms that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance. By drastically reducing the cure period, the modified text creates a potential breach of the implied duty of good faith, establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will commence on the Effective Date and continue for an Initial Term of five (5) years, and will automatically renew for additional periods of five (5) years unless one Party notifies the other of its intention not to renew, no less than 12 months prior to the expiration of the then-current term, unless terminated as permitted under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will commence on the Effective Date and continue for an Initial Term of five (5) years, and will automatically renew for additional periods of five (5) years unless one Party notifies the other of its intention not to renew, no less than 1 month prior to the expiration of the then-current term, unless terminated as permitted under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period from 12 months to 1 month could be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially considering the complex supply chain relationship. This could create significant disruption and hardship, violating general contract law principles that contracts should not be unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Unconscionability under the UCC (though this may not be a sale of goods, the principle is broadly applicable) refers to contract terms that are so one-sided as to be oppressive or unfair. A significantly shortened non-renewal period could be considered unconscionable if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements, particularly given the scale and complexity of a master supply agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Ă No thank you Â§ 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-renewal notice period from 12 months to 1 month, which introduces an inconsistency regarding time-sensitive obligations. The Perturbation Explanation argues that this change could be deemed commercially unreasonable, potentially violating general contract law principles against unconscionability. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a significantly shortened non-renewal period could be unconscionable if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from UCC Â§ 2-302, addresses unconscionable contracts, stating that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. This indicates that the reduced notice period could be challenged as unconscionable, making the modified term legally contradictory and potentially unenforceable.
